bins.name: Reform scope and FERC authority
bins.desc: ECA argues that unclear large load interconnection rules harm both large customers seeking speed to power and other customers exposed to cost shift, giving FERC ample basis to act. ECA supports broad reforms covering all transmission providers and opposes narrowing the scope to RTOs or unbundled systems only.
quotes: Commission action on large load interconnection to the transmission system is needed to ensure just and reasonable rates and prevent undue discrimination and will open new opportunities to meet the needs of new large loads for "speed to power" while protecting other customers from harm.

Large load customers like data centers, manufacturers, and industry are seeking opportunities to pay for and accelerate timelines for the generation and transmission infrastructure needed to serve them, but are stymied by inconsistent and unclear rules for large load interconnection to transmission, a lack of clear paths to line up load interconnection with the interconnection and delivery of generation they construct or contract to serve them, and fractured and incomplete transmission planning processes that fail to holistically account for projected new large loads and associated generation

ECA suggests a guidelines-based approach, requiring transmission providers across the country to demonstrate that they have in place procedures and agreements for large load interconnection to the transmission system that meet minimum guidelines prescribed by the Commission.

It is entirely reasonable for the Commission to base action here on the unremarkable theory that unprecedented growth in demand from large load customers, combined with unclear and uncertain procedures and agreements for interconnection of such loads to the transmission system that limit the development of new infrastructure to serve them, will lead to unjust and unreasonable rates and the potential for undue discrimination in the provision of transmission service.

The Commission should reject the suggestion that it limit the scope of its required reforms to only those systems where rates for transmission service are unbundled (which could be interpreted as a request to limit the reach of reforms only to Regional Transmission Organizations and Independent System Operators (RTOs/ISOs)).

bins.name: Ratepayer protection and cost transparency
bins.desc: ECA emphasizes that other customers face cost shift and stranded cost risk from large load growth, and calls for transparency in how interconnection costs are incurred and allocated. ECA opposes mandates that restrict private capital options, arguing they raise costs without benefit to existing customers.
quotes: Other customers lack sufficient transparency into how costs are incurred and allocated and in forecasts of new large loads to have confidence that investments made to serve new large loads are not being shifted to them or exposing them to stranded cost risk.

The Commission should, as a general matter, avoid taking options off the table for large customers to fund with private capital rather than ratepayer funds the new transmission and generation infrastructure needed to serve them.

Mandating that all transmission upgrades be funded by transmission providers in rate base with a return on investment, or that customers securitize network upgrades that transmission providers are funding through rate base, would lead to increased costs and less options to support speed to power without any demonstrable benefit to other customers.

bins.name: Generation and load interconnection alignment
bins.desc: ECA identifies the absence of clear pathways to align load interconnection with the generation and storage resources that large customers build or contract as a core problem requiring FERC action.
quotes: Commission action on large load interconnection to the transmission system is needed to ensure just and reasonable rates and prevent undue discrimination and will open new opportunities to meet the needs of new large loads for "speed to power" while protecting other customers from harm.

Large load customers like data centers, manufacturers, and industry are seeking opportunities to pay for and accelerate timelines for the generation and transmission infrastructure needed to serve them, but are stymied by inconsistent and unclear rules for large load interconnection to transmission, a lack of clear paths to line up load interconnection with the interconnection and delivery of generation they construct or contract to serve them, and fractured and incomplete transmission planning processes that fail to holistically account for projected new large loads and associated generation

bins.name: Flexibility and behind-meter resources
bins.desc: ECA supports greater use of behind-the-meter generation aggregation under Order 2222, including in non-RTO regions, and endorses open season models that let customers fund infrastructure or utilize off-peak capacity. ECA views these as tools that can accelerate interconnection while benefiting system reliability and all customers.
quotes: ECA strongly supports comments emphasizing the value to all customers of greater aggregation and use of behind-the-meter generation resources.

Fully implementing aggregation models under Order No. 2222, and encouraging their adoption in non-RTO regions, is critical to taking advantage of these resources to the benefit of system reliability and all customers.

ECA supports further examination of the potential for open season models to allow customers to express interest in funding new infrastructure or flexibly utilizing off-peak capacity to meet their needs.

bins.name: FERC jurisdiction and reform scope
bins.desc: ECA supports FERC's authority to regulate large load interconnection to transmission facilities without encroaching on state retail jurisdiction. ECA opposes limiting reforms to RTOs or unbundled providers and favors the seven-factor test over a voltage threshold to define jurisdictional scope.
quotes: Large load customers like data centers, manufacturers, and industry are seeking opportunities to pay for and accelerate timelines for the generation and transmission infrastructure needed to serve them, but are stymied by inconsistent and unclear rules for large load interconnection to transmission, a lack of clear paths to line up load interconnection with the interconnection and delivery of generation they construct or contract to serve them, and fractured and incomplete transmission planning processes that fail to holistically account for projected new large loads and associated generation

ECA suggests a guidelines-based approach, requiring transmission providers across the country to demonstrate that they have in place procedures and agreements for large load interconnection to the transmission system that meet minimum guidelines prescribed by the Commission.

It is entirely reasonable for the Commission to base action here on the unremarkable theory that unprecedented growth in demand from large load customers, combined with unclear and uncertain procedures and agreements for interconnection of such loads to the transmission system that limit the development of new infrastructure to serve them, will lead to unjust and unreasonable rates and the potential for undue discrimination in the provision of transmission service.

Commission regulation of the rates, terms, and conditions of interconnection to transmission facilities subject to FERC jurisdiction and related transmission service does not constitute regulation of any retail sale.

The Commission should reject the suggestion that it limit the scope of its required reforms to only those systems where rates for transmission service are unbundled (which could be interpreted as a request to limit the reach of reforms only to Regional Transmission Organizations and Independent System Operators (RTOs/ISOs)).
ECA believes continued reliance on the long-standing seven-factor test is appropriate, legally sound, and will not materially increase litigation challenges.
A voltage threshold (especially one as high as 230 kV) will be an incomplete remedy to the risks to just and reasonable rates driving the need for Commission action and leave regulatory gaps.

bins.name: Scope threshold for jurisdictional facilities
bins.desc: ECA opposes using a 230 kV voltage threshold as a jurisdictional cutoff, arguing it would exclude much of the FERC-jurisdictional system, leave regulatory gaps, and could incentivize lower-voltage construction to avoid requirements. ECA prefers the existing seven-factor test.
quotes: Other customers lack sufficient transparency into how costs are incurred and allocated and in forecasts of new large loads to have confidence that investments made to serve new large loads are not being shifted to them or exposing them to stranded cost risk.
ECA believes continued reliance on the long-standing seven-factor test is appropriate, legally sound, and will not materially increase litigation challenges.
A voltage threshold (especially one as high as 230 kV) will be an incomplete remedy to the risks to just and reasonable rates driving the need for Commission action and leave regulatory gaps.

bins.name: Customer funding and private capital options
bins.desc: ECA supports preserving all options for large customers to fund network upgrades with private capital rather than through ratepayer-funded rate base. ECA endorses open season models and opposes mandates that limit private investment pathways.
quotes: The Commission should, as a general matter, avoid taking options off the table for large customers to fund with private capital rather than ratepayer funds the new transmission and generation infrastructure needed to serve them.

Mandating that all transmission upgrades be funded by transmission providers in rate base with a return on investment, or that customers securitize network upgrades that transmission providers are funding through rate base, would lead to increased costs and less options to support speed to power without any demonstrable benefit to other customers.

ECA supports further examination of the potential for open season models to allow customers to express interest in funding new infrastructure or flexibly utilizing off-peak capacity to meet their needs.

bins.name: Behind-meter generation aggregation
bins.desc: ECA strongly supports expanding aggregation of behind-the-meter generation under Order 2222, including in non-RTO regions, arguing these resources improve reliability and reduce costs for all customers once large load customers eventually connect to the grid.
quotes: ECA strongly supports comments emphasizing the value to all customers of greater aggregation and use of behind-the-meter generation resources.

Fully implementing aggregation models under Order No. 2222, and encouraging their adoption in non-RTO regions, is critical to taking advantage of these resources to the benefit of system reliability and all customers.

bins.name: Open season capacity and funding models
bins.desc: ECA endorses open season models as a way for customers to signal willingness to pay for new infrastructure or off-peak capacity, which could accelerate grid buildout and avoid protracted cost allocation disputes.
quotes: ECA supports further examination of the potential for open season models to allow customers to express interest in funding new infrastructure or flexibly utilizing off-peak capacity to meet their needs.
